candidates.title: Equilibrium-Driven Weight Decay (EqWD) with Cumulative Alignment Contraction Theory

collisions.paper: Defazio, 2025. Why Gradients Rapidly Increase Near the End of Training. arXiv:2506.02285
collisions.overlap: Defazio establishes that gradient-to-weight ratios converge to a universal equilibrium r* across normalized layers under weight decay. EqWD uses deviation from this same r* as a WD scheduling signal. The ratio equilibrium concept is directly from Defazio.
collisions.differentiation: Defazio uses ratio as a diagnostic and proposes AdamC (correcting WD to stabilize norms). EqWD instead uses ratio DEVIATION as a modulation signal to increase/decrease WD adaptively. This is a different use case: diagnostic vs. scheduling.

collisions.paper: Xie et al., 2023 (updated 2024). On the Overlooked Pitfalls of Weight Decay (SWD). arXiv:2011.11152
collisions.overlap: SWD schedules weight decay based on gradient norm ||g_t||. EqWD uses ||g_t||/||w_t|| (ratio) instead of ||g_t|| alone. Both are gradient-statistic-driven WD schedulers.
collisions.differentiation: EqWD uses the ratio (encoding both gradient and weight magnitude) and its deviation from equilibrium, not gradient norm alone. The ratio deviation signal is structurally different from gradient norm scheduling.

collisions.paper: Li et al., ICLR 2026. Cautious Weight Decay (CWD). arXiv:2510.12402
collisions.overlap: CWD uses sign alignment between update and parameters to mask WD. Both CWD and EqWD are alignment-informed WD methods.
collisions.differentiation: CWD uses binary sign mask (no magnitude). EqWD uses continuous ratio deviation. CWD does not use gradient-to-weight ratio dynamics. Fundamentally different mechanisms.

collisions.paper: Sun et al., CVPR 2025. Investigating the Role of Weight Decay in Enhancing Nonconvex SGD
collisions.overlap: Sun provides the first generalization bound for SGDW using alignment quantity. The proposal's Theorem 1 extends Sun's worst-case bound to an average-case bound.
collisions.differentiation: Sun's bound is worst-case (delta_T = sup_t). The proposed average-case bound (delta_avg) and the formal proof of alignment-aware optimality under fixed budget are new theoretical contributions not in Sun's paper.

collisions.paper: Ghiasi et al., NeurIPS 2024. Constrained Parameter Regularization (CPR). arXiv:2311.09058
collisions.overlap: CPR applies per-matrix adaptive weight decay using Lagrangian constraints on statistical measures of weight matrices. Both CPR and EqWD are per-layer/per-matrix adaptive WD methods.
collisions.differentiation: CPR uses L2-norm constraints with augmented Lagrangian. EqWD uses gradient-to-weight ratio deviation. Different mechanisms, different theoretical grounding.

collisions.paper: He et al., NeurIPS 2025. AlphaDecay: Module-wise Weight Decay for Heavy-Tailed Balancing in LLMs. arXiv:2506.14562
collisions.overlap: AlphaDecay assigns per-module adaptive WD strengths based on spectral properties (heavy-tailed self-regularization). Both are module/layer-wise adaptive WD methods.
collisions.differentiation: AlphaDecay uses spectral density analysis (PL_Alpha_Hill) for LLM modules. EqWD uses gradient-to-weight ratio deviation. Different signals, different theoretical basis, different target architectures (LLMs vs. vision).

collisions.paper: Kosson et al., ICML 2024. Rotational Equilibrium: How Weight Decay Balances Learning Across Neural Networks. arXiv:2305.17212
collisions.overlap: Kosson establishes the rotational equilibrium framework for understanding WD dynamics, linking layer learning rates to weight decay. EqWD's equilibrium concept draws from this line of work.
collisions.differentiation: Kosson's work is analytical (understanding dynamics) not algorithmic (no WD scheduling algorithm proposed). EqWD translates equilibrium insights into a concrete scheduling algorithm.

collisions.paper: Chou, 2025. Correction of Decoupled Weight Decay. arXiv:2512.08217
collisions.overlap: Proposes corrected WD (lambda proportional to gamma^2 instead of gamma) for stable weight norms. Both address WD scheduling for stability.
collisions.differentiation: This corrects the schedule-dependence of decoupled WD (a global fix). EqWD is a per-layer adaptive method based on ratio deviation. Orthogonal contributions.

collisions.paper: Tian et al., NeurIPS 2024. Rethinking Weight Decay for Robust Fine-Tuning (Selective Projection Decay). NeurIPS 2024
collisions.overlap: SPD selectively applies WD per-layer based on gradient-parameter alignment (inner product sign). Both use gradient-parameter relationship for selective WD.
collisions.differentiation: SPD targets fine-tuning (deviation from pretrained init). EqWD targets training from scratch (ratio equilibrium deviation). SPD uses sign of inner product for binary selection; EqWD uses continuous ratio deviation magnitude.

candidates.differentiation_notes: The core novelty claim -- using gradient-to-weight ratio DEVIATION from equilibrium as a continuous WD modulation signal -- survives scrutiny. No existing method uses this specific signal. However, the novelty margin is narrower than claimed in the proposal: (1) Defazio 2025 establishes the ratio equilibrium that EqWD builds upon, and reviewers will perceive proximity; (2) Multiple concurrent works (AlphaDecay, SPD, CPR, CWD) are all doing per-layer/per-module adaptive WD with different signals, making this a crowded space; (3) The average-case alignment bound extending Sun CVPR 2025 appears genuinely novel but is an incremental theoretical extension. Key risks: reviewers may view EqWD as 'obvious next step from Defazio 2025' and the unified Phi framework as a notational exercise rather than genuine contribution. Recommend strong empirical differentiation on ImageNet.

candidates.title: BCM Sliding Threshold Weight Decay (BCM-WD)

collisions.paper: Bienenstock, Cooper, Munro, 1982. Theory of the development of neuron selectivity. J. Neuroscience.
collisions.overlap: BCM theory with sliding threshold is the foundational concept. BCM-WD directly adapts this mechanism.
collisions.differentiation: The original BCM theory is in neuroscience. Applying BCM's sliding threshold mechanism to weight decay scheduling in deep learning is a novel cross-domain transfer.

collisions.paper: Krotov et al., 2019. Unsupervised learning by competing hidden units. PNAS.
collisions.overlap: Uses BCM-like learning rules in deep learning with competition between hidden units.
collisions.differentiation: Krotov uses BCM for the learning rule itself. BCM-WD uses BCM's sliding threshold concept only for modulating weight decay strength, not for the learning rule.

collisions.paper: Li et al., ICLR 2026. Cautious Weight Decay (CWD). arXiv:2510.12402
collisions.overlap: Both use alignment-based signals to modulate WD.
collisions.differentiation: CWD uses binary sign alignment. BCM-WD uses alignment surprise relative to a sliding EMA threshold (BCM mechanism). Different inspiration and mechanism.

candidates.differentiation_notes: BCM-WD is genuinely novel: no prior work applies BCM's sliding threshold mechanism to weight decay scheduling. The cross-disciplinary transfer from synaptic plasticity to WD modulation is creative and well-differentiated. However, this candidate has lower practical impact potential: (1) the biological motivation may not resonate with ML reviewers; (2) the WD direction conflict with CWD convention needs careful resolution; (3) BCM stability conditions may not transfer to deep nets. As a backup to EqWD, this is a strong second option but harder to position for top venues.

candidates.title: Budget-Equivalent Evaluation Framework for Dynamic Weight Decay

collisions.paper: Schmidt et al., 2021. Descending through a Crowded Valley: Benchmarking Deep Learning Optimizers. arXiv:2007.01547
collisions.overlap: Comprehensive optimizer benchmarking framework with controlled comparison. Similar methodology concept.
collisions.differentiation: Schmidt et al. benchmark optimizers broadly. The proposed framework specifically targets WD methods with budget-equivalent comparison and novel metrics (BEM, CSI, AIS). Different scope and metrics.

collisions.paper: D'Angelo et al., 2024. Why Do We Need Weight Decay in Modern Deep Learning? ICLR.
collisions.overlap: Systematic empirical study of weight decay's role with controlled experiments across settings.
collisions.differentiation: D'Angelo studies WHY weight decay works (mechanism). The proposed framework compares WHICH WD method is best under budget equivalence. Different research question.

collisions.paper: Ghiasi et al., NeurIPS 2024. CPR. arXiv:2311.09058
collisions.overlap: CPR includes matched-budget comparisons with AdamW (e.g., GPT2s reaching same performance in 2/3 budget). Overlap in budget-aware evaluation methodology.
collisions.differentiation: CPR's budget comparison is incidental to their method paper. The proposed framework makes budget equivalence the central contribution with standardized metrics.

candidates.differentiation_notes: As a standalone paper, the budget-equivalent evaluation framework faces significant challenges: (1) benchmark/survey papers without novel algorithms struggle at top venues; (2) existing work (CPR, D'Angelo, Schmidt) already includes aspects of controlled WD comparison; (3) the proposed metrics (BEM, CSI, AIS) are the main novel element but may be seen as somewhat arbitrary definitions. Better positioned as a component of the EqWD paper (contributing evaluation methodology) rather than standalone. If kept standalone, a comprehensive negative finding (dynamic WD does not beat tuned fixed WD) would be the strongest positioning.

search_summary.key_finding: The WD scheduling space is more crowded than the proposal acknowledges. At least 8 concurrent/recent methods (SWD, CWD, CPR, AlphaDecay, AdamC, SPD, Amos, Correction of Decoupled WD) all address adaptive/dynamic weight decay from different angles. EqWD's specific mechanism (ratio deviation from equilibrium) remains novel, but the overall contribution space is competitive.
